name: Khabbab ibn al-Aratt
title: Khabbab ibn al-Aratt (RA): The First to Be Tortured
meta_description: The story of Khabbab ibn al-Aratt (RA): an enslaved swordsmith who was among the first to openly declare Islam, endured the harshest torture for his faith, and lived to see the victory the Prophet Muhammad promised him.
keywords: Khabbab ibn al-Aratt, story of Khabbab, first to be tortured in Islam, enslaved companion, blacksmith of Makkah, sahaba stories, the firsts
lede: He was a young enslaved boy in the slave market of Makkah, bought to make money for someone else, with no name anyone cared to know and no future anyone could see.
This is the story of Khabbab ibn al-Aratt (may Allah be pleased with him): the swordsmith who heard the call to one God, said yes before it was safe to, and paid for it with his own flesh.

paras: A woman named Umm Anmar was walking through the slave market of Makkah, the same market where so many of the famous incidents of the seerah took place. She was not only looking for help with her chores. She wanted someone she could train to earn money for her.

Among the enslaved boys she saw one who looked different: signs of intelligence, signs of strength, something distinguished in his face. She pointed and said, that one. As she walked home with him she asked his name. Khabbab, he said. He was from Banu Tamim, an Arab, taken as a child when his tribe lost a war and everything they had, their livestock, their women, their children, was carried off.
All he had ever known was slavery. He had been passed from master to master, traded from place to place. Recognising that he was capable, Umm Anmar had him trained by a blacksmith to make swords.

Khabbab learned the craft and surpassed his teacher. Still only a teenager, he became a known expert: a swordsmith with his own workshop, the man you went to if you wanted a blade made or sharpened. People knew him for his skill, his integrity, and his good character.

But none of it was his. He earned a reputation and earned nothing else. His talent built weapons for the nobles of Quraysh and wealth for the woman who owned him. He had perfected something rare, and it bought him no freedom, no money, no future of his own.
Then he heard the call of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to the oneness of God, and to the rejection of the idols and everything they stood for.

The call reached him while the Prophet ﷺ was still inviting people quietly, family by family. Khabbab did not wait for the nobles to debate it. He did not wait for the news to spread, or for it to become acceptable even among the weak and the poor of the city.

It simply made sense to him. He had not studied theology. He came to it through his nature: the oneness of God was clear, and what it meant, that people are not made to be owned and exploited, was clear too. He went straight to the Prophet ﷺ and said, extend your hand so I can pledge myself to you.

Some scholars count him as the sixth person to accept Islam, others the seventh or eighth. He is always among the first ten. And many say he was the first person to make his Islam public, and the first to be tortured for it. He hid nothing. A man famous for crafting the swords of Quraysh, with no protection in society, said openly that he had left the idols and followed Muhammad ﷺ.

When Umm Anmar heard, she sent her brother with a group of young men to find out if it was true and to deal with him if it was. They came to Khabbab at his work and said they had heard something unbelievable, that he had abandoned the religion of his people. He answered without flinching: I have not abandoned anything. I have believed in Allah, and rejected your idols, and I believe that Muhammad ﷺ is the servant and messenger of Allah.

There was quiet dignity in his answer, a refusal to let them decide who he was. He was a slave they had never valued. Now, suddenly, they cared what he believed. As soon as he spoke, they fell on him. They beat him unconscious and struck his head with the iron bars he used in his own work, until he lay bleeding in the street. Some scholars say this was the first time such a thing happened to a Muslim. He was between sixteen and eighteen years old.

When he woke, they asked again, and he said the same words again. So they starved him, lashed him, clamped him in metal under the burning sun. They experimented with ways to break him, and nothing worked. In their rage they laid burning coals on his back until the flesh cooked away. Umm Anmar would take a red-hot iron and rake it across his head. The agony made him faint. He endured all of it alone, with almost none of the Qur'an yet revealed, with no teacher and no friend beside him, holding on for the sake of Allah by himself.

sections.h2: Are you not going to call upon Allah for us?
paras: Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with him) eventually bought his freedom, as he did for many of the tortured and enslaved believers. When his father asked why he kept freeing people who brought him no benefit, Abu Bakr said he was doing it only for Allah. Khabbab was freed not to make swords, but for the sake of God, and from then on he stayed beside the Prophet ﷺ.

Even free, he had no clan to shield him, so the abuse continued. One day Khabbab came to the Prophet ﷺ, who was resting in the shade of the Kaaba, and asked, are you not going to call upon Allah for us? Are you not going to ask Him to make a way out? There was the impatience of a man who had suffered more than anyone.

The Prophet ﷺ sat up, and his face reddened. He said that before them were people combed with iron until nothing of their flesh remained on their bones, and a man sawn in two, and none of it made them leave their religion. Then he said: Allah will surely complete this matter, until a traveller can journey alone fearing no one but Allah. But you are being hasty.

There is a cruel patience to what Khabbab was being asked to trust. He might not live to see the victory at all, the Prophet ﷺ was telling him; he might die under the torture. But the faith he was tortured for would thrive, because Allah keeps His promise. Hold on.

And Khabbab lived to see it. He saw the men who had tortured him fall, one after another. The Prophet ﷺ even came to him after the battle of Badr to tell him that Allah had dealt with the one who had killed his mother. Umm Anmar, who had combed his head with hot iron, was struck with a sickness of the head so severe that in her pain she raked her own flesh the way she had once raked his, until she perished. Khabbab watched it happen.

He saw the day there was an assembly of the believers, no longer slaves in the desert under stones, but honoured companions, and the man who had once hunted them sitting among them as a leader. Once, asked about those early days, Khabbab lifted his shirt and showed his back. The flesh had never grown back. It had melted away and never fully healed, decades later still bearing what he had carried.

Years before, he had been teaching the Qur'an in a house when word came that Umar was at the door, on his way to kill the Prophet ﷺ. Khabbab hid behind a curtain, because nothing would have stopped Umar from killing him. From there he listened as Umar, having struck his own sister, sat and read the page she had been learning, and was changed by it. Khabbab stepped out and told him he hoped the prayer of the Prophet ﷺ had been answered through him. That man went on to the Prophet ﷺ and accepted Islam. The slave hiding behind the curtain had helped soften the heart the whole community had feared.

sections.h2: He never felt he was owed
paras: There was a man who owed Khabbab money, perhaps for a blade he had made, and refused to pay unless Khabbab disbelieved in Muhammad ﷺ. Khabbab told him, I will not disbelieve until you die and are resurrected. These were people who denied any life after death, and the man scoffed: then I will have wealth and children there too, and I will pay you then. About that arrogance Allah revealed a verse:

In his last days Khabbab became wealthy and generous, and it frightened him. He wept, remembering companions who had died with nothing, their reward untouched and waiting. He feared that he had been given his good things here, in this life, and that nothing would be left for him with Allah.

Think about who was saying this. A man whose back had been burned to the bone for his faith was afraid he had not done enough. If Khabbab did not feel entitled, after all of that, then none of us are owed anything. Everything he endured had been for Allah, and he held no claim over Him for it.

ayah.text: Have you considered the man who rejects Our revelation, who says, 'I will certainly be given wealth and children'?

lessons_intro: His story is not one heroic moment. It is a young man holding the same answer, over and over, while everything was done to make him let go.

lessons.text: Khabbab had no learning and no standing. The oneness of God simply made sense to his nature, and he answered before it was safe. Sincerity does not wait for permission.
Beaten, starved, and burned, he repeated the same words each time he woke. What we believe is proven not when it is easy, but when it costs us something.

lessons.bold: Trust the timing of the outcome.
lessons.text: The Prophet ﷺ told him relief was certain but he was being hasty. The believer is patient, watches the plan unfold, and does not demand that it arrive on his own schedule.

lessons.bold: Stay humble before your own good.
lessons.text: After all he suffered, Khabbab feared he had been given too much, not too little. He held no sense of being owed. Neither should we.

faq.a: An early companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. He was an enslaved Arab from Banu Tamim, trained as a swordsmith in Makkah, who was among the first to accept Islam and is often described as the first to declare it openly and the first to be tortured for it.

faq.q: Why is Khabbab remembered for torture?
faq.a: Because he had no clan to protect him and refused to hide his faith, his owners and others abused him without restraint: beating, starvation, lashing, hot metal under the sun, and burning coals laid on his back. Decades later the scars on his back had still not healed.

faq.q: What did the Prophet ﷺ say to him when he asked for relief?
faq.a: He reminded Khabbab of believers before him who endured even worse and never abandoned their faith, and he promised that Allah would complete this matter until a lone traveller could journey in safety. He also told him he was being hasty.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Khabbab?
faq.a: That sincere faith can reach anyone, that conviction is proven under pressure, that we should trust the timing of God's promise rather than rush it, and that even a life of great sacrifice need not breed a sense of entitlement.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Khabbab had every reason to feel the world owed him something, and he felt the opposite. A man whose body had been cooked for his faith worried, at the end, that he had been given too much. That is not weakness. It is a heart that never made its worship into a transaction.

Most of us will never be tested the way he was. But the impatience he felt in the shade of the Kaaba is familiar: the quiet demand that relief come now, that our patience be rewarded on our terms. His life gently asks us to hold our answer steady, to trust that the outcome is in better hands than ours, and to stop keeping a ledger with the One we are serving. Carry that into one hard moment today, and his story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Khabbab ibn al-Aratt (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Qur'an translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Qur'an 19:77). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
A young enslaved boy in the slave market of Makkah.

Bought to make money for someone else.

No future anyone could see.

This is Khabbab ibn al-Aratt.

He became a master swordsmith with his own workshop.

The nobles of Quraysh came to him for their blades.

He earned a reputation, and kept nothing. The work was not his, and neither was he.

Then he heard the call to one God.

He did not wait for the nobles to debate it. He did not wait for it to be safe.

He went straight to the Prophet ﷺ: extend your hand, so I can pledge myself to you.

He hid nothing.

When they came to rough him up, he answered: I have believed in Allah, and rejected your idols.

They beat him unconscious with the iron bars from his own workshop. He was barely seventeen.

They starved him. They lashed him. They burned him under the sun.

Then they laid hot coals on his back until the flesh cooked away.

He endured it alone, holding the same answer each time he woke.

Worn down, he asked the Prophet ﷺ: are you not going to call upon Allah for us?

The Prophet's face reddened. He spoke of believers sawn in two who never left their faith, and said: Allah will complete this matter. But you are being hasty.

And he lived to see it.

Decades later, honoured among the believers, he lifted his shirt to show his back.

The flesh had never grown back. He had carried it the whole way.

**He never felt he was owed.**

At the end he grew wealthy, and it frightened him. He wept, afraid he had been given too much.

If Khabbab did not feel entitled, none of us are.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
Khabbab ibn al-Aratt (may Allah be pleased with him) was an enslaved boy bought from the Makkan slave market to earn money for his owner. He grew into a master swordsmith, and kept nothing for himself.

When he heard the call to one God, he did not wait for it to become safe. He declared his faith openly, and was among the first to be tortured for it: beaten, starved, burned with coals laid on his back. He held the same answer each time he woke.

He lived long enough to see the religion he suffered for prevail. And at the end, having grown wealthy, he wept, afraid he had been given too much in this life.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/khabbab-ibn-al-aratt

email.subject: He was burned for his faith, and feared he was given too much
email.alt_subject: The boy in the slave market who would not let go
email.preview: Khabbab ibn al-Aratt declared his faith before it was safe, and paid for it with his own flesh.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

He began as an enslaved boy in the slave market of Makkah, bought to earn money for someone else. His name was Khabbab ibn al-Aratt (may Allah be pleased with him), and he had no future that anyone could see.

He grew into a master swordsmith. The nobles of Quraysh came to him for their blades. He earned a fine reputation and kept nothing of it, because the work was not his and neither was he.

Then he heard the call to the oneness of God. He did not wait for the nobles to debate it, or for it to become safe even among the poor of the city. It simply made sense to him. He went straight to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and said, extend your hand so I can pledge myself to you. He hid nothing.

When they came to deal with him, he answered plainly: I have believed in Allah, and rejected your idols. They beat him unconscious with the iron bars from his own workshop. He was perhaps seventeen. Then they starved him, lashed him, burned him under the sun, and laid hot coals on his back until the flesh cooked away. He held the same answer each time he woke, alone, with almost none of the Qur'an yet revealed and no friend beside him.

Once, worn down, he asked the Prophet ﷺ if he would not call upon Allah for relief. The Prophet's face reddened. He spoke of believers before them who were sawn in two and never left their faith, and he promised that Allah would complete this matter. Then he said: but you are being hasty.

And Khabbab lived to see it. Decades later, honoured among the believers, he lifted his shirt to show a back whose flesh had never grown back.

There is a lesson in him that is easy to miss. At the end he grew wealthy, and it frightened him. He wept, afraid he had been given too much in this life and that nothing was left for him with Allah. A man whose body had been burned for his faith did not feel he was owed a thing.

May Allah be pleased with him, and make us patient the way he was patient.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: He was a young enslaved boy in the slave market of Makkah, bought by a woman who wanted someone she could train to earn money for her. She noticed signs of intelligence and strength in his face and pointed at him: that one. His name was Khabbab, and he had been taken as a child when his tribe lost a war. All he had ever known was slavery.

She had him trained as a swordsmith, and he became a master, with his own workshop, sought out by the nobles of Quraysh. He earned a reputation and kept nothing. The work built weapons for others and wealth for the woman who owned him, and left him with no future of his own.

Then he heard the call to one God, and he did not wait. He went straight to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and pledged himself, before it was safe to, before the news had even spread. And he hid nothing. When men came to deal with him, he told them plainly: I have believed in Allah, and rejected your idols.

They beat him unconscious with the iron from his own workshop. He was barely a man. They starved him, lashed him, burned him under the sun, and laid hot coals on his back until the flesh cooked away. Each time he woke, he gave the same answer. He endured it alone, with almost none of the Qur'an yet revealed and no one beside him to teach or comfort him.

Worn down at last, he came to the Prophet ﷺ in the shade of the Kaaba and asked, are you not going to call upon Allah for us? The Prophet's face reddened. He spoke of believers before them who were combed with iron and sawn in two and never left their faith, and he promised that Allah would complete this matter, until a lone traveller could journey in safety. But you are being hasty, he said.

And Khabbab lived to see it. He saw the men who had tortured him fall, and the day the believers sat honoured where once they had been slaves under stones. Asked about those days, he lifted his shirt and showed a back whose flesh had never grown back.

At the end of his life he grew wealthy, and it frightened him. He wept, afraid he had been given too much in this world, that he had taken his reward here and left nothing for the next. A man whose body had been burned for his faith did not feel that Allah owed him anything at all.
storybook.pullquote: I have believed in Allah, and I have rejected your idols.
storybook.reflection: Khabbab held one answer steady while everything was done to make him let go, and then, at the end, feared he had been given too much rather than too little. His life asks us for two hard things: to trust that the outcome will come in its own time, not ours, and to serve without ever keeping a ledger of what we think we are owed.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
